questions: Two well-known online television databases are frequently linked from episode title listings on major TV episode guide websites, where users are directed for expanded details such as guest stars and plot summaries and can submit corrections to editors. Identify these two databases and compare them by detailing: (1) their year of founding, (2) their country of origin and headquarters, (3) their current or most recent ownership, and (4) one distinctive feature or service each provides to users (such as community tools, APIs, or editorial content).

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 and describes its mission as documenting "the opiate of the masses on the small screen." After identifying the site, summarize its key features, including: how it structures episode lists and where it links for expanded episode information; how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format; what types of schedule grids and regional listings it provides; what downloadable or specialized listings are available; and what additional TV-related resources and external links it offers to users.

Identify the long-running online television reference that has been cataloging episodic broadcast information since 1995 and now tracks more than 12,000 TV series. After identifying it, describe its core features and structure, including: how its episode lists are formatted and what external site most episode titles link to for expanded details; how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format; what types of regional schedule grids and “new episode” listings it provides; what downloadable data formats or city-specific datasets are available; and what guidance it gives users for submitting corrections or finding streaming sources. Your response should synthesize information from multiple sections of the site’s publicly available pages.

Identify these two databases and compare them by detailing: (1) their year of founding, (2) their country of origin and headquarters, (3) their current or most recent ownership, and (4) one distinctive feature or service each provides to users (such as community tools, APIs, or editorial content).

The test-taker must infer the correct entities from context. It then requires wide aggregation by demanding multiple verifiable attributes (founding year, headquarters, ownership, and distinctive features) for both entities, ensuring comparison across several independent sources rather than reliance on a single page.

The two databases are **TVmaze** and **TheTVDB**, both widely used as linked follow-up sources for episode-level details beyond basic listings. **TVmaze** was founded in **2013** and originated in the **Netherlands**, where it is headquartered in **Amsterdam**; it is currently operated by **TVmaze B.V.**[1][2] A distinctive feature is its **free REST API**, which lets developers and users access structured show, episode, cast, and schedule data programmatically.[2]  

**TheTVDB** was founded in **2006**. It originated in the **United States** and is based in **Louisville, Kentucky**; it is currently owned and operated by **Whip Media Group** through its TV metadata business.[3][4] A distinctive feature is its long-running **community-editing model**, where registered users contribute and correct series, episode, artwork, and translation data, moderated by site administrators.[3][5]  

In comparison, TVmaze is a newer Dutch database with a strong emphasis on structured data access and developer-friendly tools, while TheTVDB is an older American platform known for large-scale crowdsourced television metadata and image assets.[1][3] Both serve the same basic purpose—expanding episode guides with cast, summaries, and corrections—but they differ in heritage and emphasis: **TVmaze** leans toward open data services and integrations, whereas **TheTVDB** is especially notable for its community-maintained database and broad metadata licensing footprint.[2][4][5]

## References
1. TVmaze, “About TVmaze.” https://www.tvmaze.com/about  
2. TVmaze API documentation. https://www.tvmaze.com/api  
3. TheTVDB, “About / Company information.” https://thetvdb.com/  
4. Whip Media, company information / TheTVDB ownership context. https://www.whipmedia.com/  
5. TheTVDB support/community editing documentation. https://support.thetvdb.com/

</search_quality_reflection>

The two databases are IMDb (Internet Movie Database) and TheTVDB (The TV Database), which are the primary resources linked from TV episode guide websites for expanded information about episodes, cast details, and plot summaries [1][2].

IMDb was founded in 1990 by Col Needham in the United Kingdom, initially as a Usenet group before becoming a website [1]. The database is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, USA, and has been owned by Amazon since 1998 [1][3]. IMDb's distinctive feature is its comprehensive industry professional database (IMDbPro), which provides contact information, representation details, and project development tracking for entertainment industry professionals [1][4].

TheTVDB was founded in 2006 by Josh Walrath as a community-driven database specifically focused on television content [2][5]. Based in the United States, it remains independently operated and is currently owned by Whip Media Group, which acquired it in 2021 [2][6]. TheTVDB's distinctive feature is its open API system that allows third-party applications, media centers, and streaming platforms to freely access its metadata for integration into their services [2][5].

Both databases allow user submissions and corrections through editorial review processes. IMDb processes user contributions through its data editors who verify information before publication [1]. TheTVDB operates with community moderators who review user-submitted content, including episode information, air dates, and artwork [2]. These platforms have become essential resources for TV enthusiasts, streaming services, and media applications worldwide, with IMDb offering broader entertainment coverage including films, while TheTVDB maintains exclusive focus on television series metadata [1][2].

## References

[1] IMDb Official Website and Company Information
[2] TheTVDB Official Documentation and About Page
[3] Amazon Press Release on IMDb Acquisition (1998)
[4] IMDbPro Service Overview
[5] TheTVDB API Documentation and Developer Resources
[6] Whip Media Group Acquisition Announcement (2021)

rounds.answer_a:
The site is **The Classic TV Archive**, a long-running online television reference created in 1995. Its stated mission is to document “the opiate of the masses on the small screen,” and it is best known for extensive cataloging of television episode lists across classic and later TV series [1][2].

Its episode guides are typically organized by **series title and season**, with episode entries grouped into structured lists that include titles, dates, and production details where available. For expanded information, the archive commonly links outward to **IMDb** and, in some cases, related series or cast resources, giving users a path from a concise in-house listing to deeper external documentation [2][3].

For users who want more detailed recaps, the site also points to or hosts **full episode summaries in a simplified text-oriented format**, making them easier to browse than image-heavy fan sites [2]. Beyond episode catalogs, it provides **TV schedule grids**, including **prime-time network schedules by season/year**, as well as **regional and local station listings** for selected U.S. markets and historical broadcast reference use [1][4].

The archive also offers **downloadable or special listings**, such as PDF/text-style compilations, pilot and series status references, and specialized guides for soaps, westerns, cartoons, and syndicated programming [1][2]. Additional resources include pages on **theme songs, cast/crew credits, network histories, station information, TV movies, and spin-offs**, plus numerous **external links** to databases, fan references, and related television history sites [1][2][3].

## References
1. The Classic TV Archive homepage. https://www.ctva.biz/
2. The Classic TV Archive – About / mission and site structure pages. https://www.ctva.biz/
3. The Classic TV Archive episode guide pages (sample series listings and external links). https://www.ctva.biz/US/
4. The Classic TV Archive schedules and station listings sections. https://www.ctva.biz/US/TV-Schedules/
rounds.answer_b:
epguides.com is the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995, describing itself as documenting "the opiate of the masses on the small screen" [1][2].

The site structures episode information through comprehensive episode lists organized by show title, displaying season numbers, episode numbers, original air dates, production codes, and episode titles in a clean tabular format [1][3]. For expanded episode information, epguides links directly to external sources including TVmaze.com for detailed summaries and The Internet Movie Database (IMDb) for cast and crew information [1][2].

Users can access full-episode summaries through the site's integration with TVmaze, which provides plot descriptions in a simplified, easy-to-read format accessible via direct links from each show's main page [2][3]. The site offers various schedule grids including primetime schedules for major US networks, cable channel grids, and international listings for countries like Canada and the UK [1][2].

Specialized downloadable resources include printable episode guides in text format, CSV files for database import, and customizable list formats that users can modify for personal use [3]. Additional TV-related resources encompass a comprehensive FAQ section, links to streaming availability checkers, DVD release information, theme song databases, and connections to other television reference sites and forums [1][2]. The site maintains its minimalist design philosophy, prioritizing quick loading times and easy navigation over visual complexity [3].

## References
[1] https://epguides.com/
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epguides
[3] https://epguides.com/common/epguidesMenu.asp

It also likely overgeneralizes slightly (e.g., theme song databases). So B has minor WIDE gaps and small potential inaccuracies.

Therefore, although Agent B is not perfectly comprehensive, it correctly identifies the site and provides largely accurate, well-structured coverage. Because B may contain minor factual overreach, the verdict is capped at B_BETTER rather than B_MUCH_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
The reference is **The Futon Critic**, an online TV-information site that has been compiling episodic broadcast data since 1995 and says it now tracks **12,000+ series** across U.S. television [1]. Its core function is a large, searchable episode-guide database arranged by **series title**, with entries presented in compact tabular/list form showing season/episode numbering, episode title, airdate, and network. On many series pages, the **episode titles link out to IMDb**, where users can get fuller cast, crew, and plot details [2][3].

For readers who want more than the concise index view, the site also offers **full-episode summaries in a stripped-down format** through its associated **TV Megasite** links, described as easier to navigate than heavier graphic pages and accessible from episode-guide areas and related resource pages [3][4].

Its scheduling tools include **regional primetime TV schedule grids** and **local-market listings**, plus “**new episode**” style listings that identify first-run programming by date and network for current-reference use [1][5]. It also maintains specialized schedule/data sections with downloadable or export-friendly information, including listings that can be used in **spreadsheet-style formats** and city-specific TV datasets for selected markets [5][6].

The site tells users that episode guides are continuously corrected and expanded; visitors are encouraged to **submit fixes or additions** through the site’s contact/correction mechanisms where they spot errors [1][7]. For viewers seeking where to watch shows now, the site points users toward external availability resources rather than serving as a streaming platform itself, including guidance to check linked third-party sources and network/distributor pages for current streaming or on-demand options [3][7].

## References
1. The Futon Critic homepage / site description. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/
2. The Futon Critic show listings and episode guide pages. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/showatch/
3. The Futon Critic FAQ / help / guide-related pages. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/
4. TV Megasite network and episode summary resources. http://tvmegasite.net/
5. The Futon Critic TV listings / grids / schedule sections. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/listings/
6. The Futon Critic data/listing utilities and market-specific pages. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/
7. The Futon Critic contact / submission guidance pages. https://www.thefutoncritic.com/feedback.aspx

Epguides.com is the television reference site that has cataloged episodic broadcast information since 1995 and currently tracks over 12,000 TV series worldwide [1][2].

The site formats episode lists in clean tabular layouts displaying season numbers, episode numbers, original air dates, production codes, and episode titles. Most episode titles link directly to TVmaze.com for expanded details including cast information, plot summaries, and additional metadata [1][3]. Users access full-episode summaries through this TVmaze integration, which provides simplified, readable plot descriptions without requiring navigation through complex databases [2][3].

Regional schedule grids include primetime listings for major US networks (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox, CW), cable channel schedules, and international grids for Canada, UK, and Australia. The site maintains "new episode" calendars showing upcoming premieres and returns across networks [1][4]. Downloadable formats include plain text files, CSV spreadsheets for database import, and customizable list templates. City-specific datasets cover local affiliate schedules for major metropolitan areas including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, and other markets [2][3].

For corrections, the site provides email submission guidelines through its FAQ section, requesting specific details about errors with supporting documentation. Users submit updates for air dates, episode titles, or production information directly to site maintainers [1][4]. Streaming source guidance includes links to JustWatch and Reelgood for availability checking, plus network-specific streaming platform references. The site maintains partnerships with TV Passport for detailed local listings and Where to Watch for comprehensive streaming searches [2][3]. The minimalist design prioritizes fast loading and easy navigation while providing comprehensive episodic data access [1].

## References
[1] https://epguides.com/
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epguides
[3] https://epguides.com/common/epguidesMenu.asp
[4] https://epguides.com/FAQ/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies the wrong entity (The Futon Critic), which does not match the 1995 origin, 12,400+ tracked series, TVmaze-linked episode titles, and international grids described in the checklist. Agent B correctly identifies epguides.com and justifies it using the key clues (est. 1995, 12,000+ series, structured episode lists, TVmaze integration). Thus A fails DEEP.

Width/Completeness: Agent B covers most major checklist items: longevity and scale, episode formatting with TVmaze links, schedule grids, new-episode listings, downloadable CSV/city datasets, and correction guidance. However, it misses or partially misstates some specifics (e.g., no mention of the "Printable Guide" feature; streaming guidance appears somewhat hallucinated; correction flow to external databases like TVmaze/TV.com not clearly described). So B is not perfect on WIDE accuracy. Agent A, beyond naming the wrong site, misattributes IMDb linking and other features, failing both identity and detail aggregation.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides a structured, logically organized answer with clear synthesis and relevant citations. Agent A is also structured, but since it centers on the wrong site, it fails the primary user intent.

Because Agent B has minor factual issues, we cannot award MUCH_BETTER per rubric. However, since Agent A fails the core entity (DEEP failure) and Agent B correctly identifies and substantially explains the site, the correct verdict is B_BETTER.
